summary: Gruber highlights Apple's newsroom piece detailing how the Digital Markets Act is causing EU users to miss out on features like Live Translation with AirPods and iPhone Mirroring. He argues Apple structured the piece effectively by leading with the consumer harm. He then amplifies Apple's argument that the DMA is failing its stated goals of promoting competition and choice, instead reducing differentiation and creating unfair conditions. His central rhetorical move is a thought experiment: if Apple switched EU iPhones to Android, all DMA conflicts would vanish — but consumer choice would be destroyed, not enhanced.
key_points: EU users are losing access to features like Live Translation and iPhone Mirroring because the DMA requires Apple to make them work on non-Apple devices first
Apple argues the DMA is achieving the opposite of its goals: fewer choices, less differentiation, and unfair competition that only targets Apple while Samsung leads EU market share
Gruber's thought experiment — switching EU iPhones to Android — crystallizes how the DMA paradoxically penalizes the existence of a competing platform
The DMA's interoperability requirements effectively punish Apple for building an integrated ecosystem, while competitors who copied those innovations face no restrictions
iOS's exclusive features and Continuity integrations are themselves a form of competition that the DMA undermines
money_quotes: If Apple were to just switch the iPhone's OS from iOS to Android, these DMA conflicts would all go away.
The DMA is supposedly intended to increase 'competition', which in turn should increase consumer choice. But the easiest way for Apple to comply with the DMA would be to switch EU iPhones to Android.
EU consumers would effectively have no choice at all in mobile OSes. They'd just get to choose which brand of Android phone to buy.
How in the world would that increase competition? iOS's unique and exclusive features — which, yes, in many cases, are exclusive to the Apple device ecosystem — are competition.
Live Translation with AirPods and iPhone Mirroring are both amazing features. And EU users are missing out on them.
I think Apple structured this piece exactly right, by emphasizing first that the most direct effect of the DMA is that EU users are getting great features late — or never.
key_insight: The DMA's interoperability mandates paradoxically punish the existence of a competing platform, and the logical endpoint of full compliance — switching to Android — would eliminate consumer choice rather than expand it.
